content.full_text:
Re:
Fern Por-rrrcAl PnncrrcEs CoMMrssroN
-128 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacrantento. CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) -122-0886
September 23,2009
James N. Fincher
County Counsel
County of Merced
2222M Street, #309
Merced, CA 95338
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-191
Dear Mr. Fincher:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the County of
Merced under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
We provide informal assistance because you are not asking advice for a specific official,
but rather seek advice on a matter on which you have a duty to advise the county.
lnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an
opinion or formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3).)
Moreover, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not
address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law
conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. (Regulation 18329(b)(8XD).)
QI]ESTION
May Merced County contract with a law firm to perform public defender duties,
and, if so, is there a conflict of interest for the District Attomey, when the firm is partially
owned by the spouse of the District Attomey?
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory ret'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Crde of Regulations. All regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


Our File No. I-09-19l
Page2
CONCLUSION
The Act does not restrict the ability of the county to enter into such a contract. If
the contract is approved, under most circumstances, the District Attorney would not have
a conflict of interest in making govemmental decisions in running his office. However,
he may have a conflict of interest if major policy decisions have a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on the firm, as explained below.
FACTS
Merced County is legally required to provide criminal defense counsel to indigent
defendants. In most cases, the representation is provided through the Office of the
County Public Defender (PD). However, in some circumstances the public defender is
disqualified from representing criminal defendants due to conflicts of interest -- for
example, in certain cases involving multiple defendants. In such cases, the County,s
obligation to provide counsel remains. The County meets this obligation by contracting
with private attomeys to act as "conflict public defenders." The pu-blic Defender refers
approximately 50 cases a month to the "conflict public defenders."
Your office is currently reviewing the extension of a "conflict public defender"
contract with Merced Defense Associates (the firm) which is wholly owned by Morse &
Pfeiff, a partnership owned by two attorneys, Cindy Morse and Thomas pfeiff. Cindy
Morse is the spouse of Larry Morse, who is currently the Merced District Attorney (DA).
Ms' Morse does not practice criminal law and the conflict PD cases will be handted by 
.
her partner Thomas Pfeiff. In addition, you understand that Ms. Morse will have an
ethical wall between her and the PD cases. However, as a partner in the firm, she will
receive a share of the profits from the contract.
The contract provides that the county will pay MDA a flat annual fee of gl.6
million, regardless of the number or type of cases referred to MDA. Because of the flat
fee, MDA's profits or losses will be driven by the expenses of MDA in performing the
contract. MDA in turn hires contract attomeys to handle each case, also based on a flat
fee' Thus, if the District Attorney wgre to litigate fewer cases, presumably fewer cases
would be defended by the public defenders office, and fewer cases would result in
conflicts of interests to be referred to MDA. Likewise, the District Attorney could shift
resources to gang cases, which create more frequent conflicts of interest for the public
Defenders Office. However, the current Public Defender stated that he doubts that policy
decisions of the District Attomey's office would have a significant effect on the number
of conflict referrals, since conflicts generally depends on facts and circumstances of each
particular case.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests


Our File No. I-09-191
Page 3
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section S1001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmlntal
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Steps I and 2: Is the District Attorney a public official who will be making,
participating in, or influencing a governmental decision?
Your question concerns a contract for public defender services and the
consequences of contracting with a firm in which your District Attorney has an interest
(as discussed further below). District Attomeys are public officials. (Section 82048;
Section 87200.) There is also no dispute that the District Attomey will be making
litigation decisions.
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contrictual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. Making a governmental decision includes
voting on a matter or committing the agency to a course of action. (Regulation
l87o2.l(a).) Generally, making a litigation decision (e.g., amendment of pleadings;
commencement of additional litigation; making and responding to discovery requests;
case management; making and responding to pre-trial motions; engaging in trial strategy
and tactics; making and responding to settlement offers; and appealing district court
decisions) is considered to be making a govprnmental decision for purposes of the Act.)
(Randolph Advice Letter, No. A-01-109.)
Step 3. What are the DA's economic interests?
Your facts reveal the following economic interests.
An indirect investment in a business entity. (Section g7103(a).) An indirect
investment means any investment owned by the spouse of an official or by a
member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public
official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's
immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a l0-
percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family," is defined at
Section 82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months priorlo the
time in which the relevant govemmental decision was made. (Section gltOE(");
Regulation 18703.3.) This includes community property income. (section
82030.) Pursuant to secrion 87103(c) and 82030, this also includes a pro rata
share of income from a client of the business if the official's direct or indirect
(through the spouse) ownership interest in the business is ten percent or more.


Our File No. I-09-191
Page 4
o d public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances and
those of his or her immediate family - this is the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) A public official always has an economic
interest in his or her personal finances, and may have a conflict of interest in any
decision resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income,
assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family. (Section 87103;
- 
Regulation 18703.5.)
Step 4: Will the DA's economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in the
decisions?
Business Entities and Sources of Income
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved
in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or indirectly by
an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.', (Regulation
18704.1(a).)
While it may appear that the firm, as a business entity and source of income
economic interest, would be directly involved in the decision because the firm's agents
(contract attomeys) appear before the DA, in fact their appearance is only in a
representative capacity, as an agent ofa defendant. The client is the actual applicant and
named party.' The firm which is the source of income to the District Attorney is not
directly involved in the decision. (Gill Advice Letter, No. 4-85-252.) However, under
these circumstances, the firm will be considered indirectly involved in the decision.
Personal Finances
The personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family are
always deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision if the decision will
have any financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family. (Regulation 18704.5)
r Contrast this situation with the county's initial decision to contract with the firm, or a future
decision to terminate, modify the terms, or extend the contract. In those cases, the firm would be the
applicant and the tjrm would be directly involved in that decision.


Our File No. I-09-191
Page 5
Step 5: What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation I 8700(a).)
When a business in which an official has an investment interest, and which is also
a source of income to the official is indirectly involved in a govemmental decision, the
appropriate materiality standard would be found in Regulation 1g705.1. (See,
Regulations 18705.1 and 18705.3(bXl).) The particular materiality standard to apply
depends on the size of the entity. (See, Regulation 18705.1.) For a relatively small
business that is not publically traded, which we assume the DA's spouse's law firm is,
Regulation I 8705. 1(c)(4) provides:
"[T]he financial effect of a govemmental decision on the business
entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $20,000 or more; or,
. 
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more."
Effects on an official's personal finances are material as stated in Regulation
I 8705.5(a), which provides:
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's
personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month period.
when determining whether a governmental decision hur u material
financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her
personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property
owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on ttre
gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business
entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall
be considered."
Therefore, governmental decision that affects the DA's personal income, or that
of his immediate family, by $250 (excluding effects on gross revenues, expenses, or
value of assets and liabilities of the firm) or more in any l2-month period is considered a


Our File No. t-09-l9l
Page 6
material financial effect. However, since the facts you present do not raise an issue
regarding a personal financial effect on the DA, beyond the effect on his spouse's firm,
we will focus our analysis only on the effect on his spouse's firm, we williocus our
analysis only on the effect on the firm as set forth in Regulation 18705.l(cXa) above.
step 6: Is it reasonably foreseeabre that the financial effect of the
governmental decisions on your economic interests will meet the applicable
materiality standard?
An effect meets the "reasonably foreseeable" threshold if the effect is
*substantiallylikely." (Regulation 18706; InreThorner(1975) l FppCops. l9g.) A
financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the
other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reurorro6ly foreseeable.
In applying this standard, you must consider whether any of the DA's decisions
regarding prosecutions by his office will foreseeably and materially financially affect the
law firm. For example, since the contract provides a flat fee for the firm, the inquiry will
be whether a given decision will save the firm money in performing the contract, and
then whether the savings will be material under the thresholds set forttr in Regulation
18705.1.
As a general proposition, we think it is difficult to say it is substantially likely that
any given decision by the DA, whether on a single prosecution, or a generat poticy 
-
affecting all, or particular types of prosecutions, wiil have a given finlancial impact on his
spouse's law firm. Therefore, under these facts, we do not think that most decisions
made by the DA will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his
economic interests. However, it is conceivable that certain policy decisions by the DA
could foreseeably have a financial impact on his spouse's firm that could have a financial
impact on his spouse's firm that could have a $20,000 effect on the firm's assets or gross
revenues for a fiscal year, or a $5,000 effect on expenses. For example, if the DA were to
decide substantially reduce the number of cases prosecuted or focus iris office's resources
on the types of prosecutions that resulted in charges against multiple defendants, and thus
a clear increase in the number of cases referred to his spouse's firm,, there would be a
foreseeable financial impact on the firm, so while, it appears the DA would generally not
have a conflict of interest in making day-to day decisions, there may be situ;ions where
a major policy decision could create a conflict of interest.
steps 7 and 8: Public Generally and Legally Required participation
Exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(6X7) and 1g707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87 103; Regulation I g70g).


Our File No. I-09-191
PageT
You have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable
to your situation.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(et6) 322-s660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
rl t'J*ltlo-
o
w
By: John Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
Jwwjgl

financial effect on the firm, as explained below.

existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
